content.text: The executive steering committee (ESC) is the governance body that gives the GenAI programme its organisational authority. Without a properly constituted ESC, every contested decision — funding a Phase 3 investment, killing an underperforming use case, overruling a BU that wants to bypass the CoE's risk framework — escalates to informal channels and either stalls or gets resolved inconsistently. The ESC structure converts ad hoc politics into a defined governance mechanism.
Why the Steering Committee Is Not Optional
GenAI programmes fail in three ways that only executive governance can prevent:
— When GenAI competes with other IT and digital investments for capital, a programme without executive sponsorship loses to programmes whose sponsors sit at the budget table.
— When the CoE's standards conflict with a powerful BU's preferences, only an executive body with authority over both can resolve the dispute. The CoE Lead cannot overrule a BU GM; the ESC can.
Risk decisions above the CoE's authority

— Tier 3 use cases (high-risk, customer-facing, consequential decisions) require sign-off from executives who own the regulatory and reputational risk. These decisions cannot be delegated to the CoE Lead.

The steering committee is not a progress-report audience — it is a decision-making body. Its agenda should consist of decisions, not updates. If every ESC meeting is a status presentation with no votes taken, the committee is decorative, not functional.
The ESC should have 5–8 members. Fewer than 5 concentrates authority too narrowly; more than 8 makes decision-making slow and attendance unreliable. Each member has a specific reason for being there:
Why They Must Be on the ESC
Technical authority and programme ownership; able to make platform investment decisions; peer relationship with other C-suite
Chair: convenes, sets agenda, breaks ties
Budget release authority at each phase gate; cost governance accountability; investment thesis validation
Phase gate budget approval; monthly cost variance sign-off
Enterprise risk and security accountability; Tier 3 risk use-case approval; mandatory suspension authority
Tier 3 security risk approval; emergency suspension
Legal accountability for regulatory compliance; data processing agreement sign-off; employment law review for HR AI use cases
Tier 3 legal risk approval; regulatory compliance sign-off
Business demand representation; use-case prioritisation; ensures BU accountability for adoption targets
BU use-case priority disputes; BU resource commitment
Chief Data Officer (if distinct from CDO)
Data governance and data quality accountability; data architecture decisions
Data access policy; data quality standards
AI impact on workforce; change management; employment implications of automation use cases
— Attends all ESC meetings; presents programme reports; executes ESC decisions. Not a voting member because the CoE Lead reports to the committee.
— Provides independent risk assessment for Tier 3 use cases; does not vote on use-case approvals to maintain independence.
— Attends when regulatory matters are on the agenda; provides legal opinion, not business judgment.

The ESC must have explicitly documented decision rights. Without this, every decision becomes a negotiation about who has authority to make it. The following matrix covers the most common contested decision categories:
Phase gate approval (advance programme to next phase)
Kill / sunset decision for active use case
Emergency suspension of a use case
The ESC meets quarterly for strategy and phase gate reviews, with extraordinary sessions triggered by specific events. The CoE Lead convenes extraordinary sessions; the ESC Chair approves the agenda.
ESC acknowledges; flags any variances requiring action
Advance / Hold / Pause decision voted on
Any At-Risk use cases escalated to ESC; sunset decisions if needed
Variance approval; phase budget release if gate passed
Open risk items; regulatory developments; any new Tier 3 implications
Agenda for next quarter set; action items assigned with owners and due dates
Active security incident with business impact (CISO convenes within 24 hours)
Regulatory enforcement action or regulatory inquiry involving a GenAI use case
Request to kill a use case that the BU GM is formally disputing
Executive sponsor departure requiring committee membership change
Phase gate crisis — use case batch failure requiring programme re-scope

# ESC meeting tracker and action item management
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from datetime import date
from enum import Enum

class ActionStatus(Enum):
    OPEN      = "open"
    IN_FLIGHT = "in_flight"
    COMPLETE  = "complete"
    OVERDUE   = "overdue"

@dataclass
class ActionItem:
    id: str
    description: str
    owner: str
    due_date: date
    status: ActionStatus = ActionStatus.OPEN
    completion_note: str = ""

    def check_status(self, as_of: date) -> ActionStatus:
        if self.status == ActionStatus.COMPLETE:
            return ActionStatus.COMPLETE
        if as_of > self.due_date and self.status != ActionStatus.COMPLETE:
            return ActionStatus.OVERDUE
        return self.status

@dataclass
class ESCMeeting:
    meeting_date: date
    attendees: list[str]
    decisions: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)
    action_items: list[ActionItem] = field(default_factory=list)

    def overdue_actions(self, as_of: date) -> list[ActionItem]:
        return [a for a in self.action_items if a.check_status(as_of) == ActionStatus.OVERDUE]

    def open_actions(self, as_of: date) -> list[ActionItem]:
        return [a for a in self.action_items if a.check_status(as_of) in [ActionStatus.OPEN, ActionStatus.IN_FLIGHT]]

    def meeting_pack_summary(self) -> str:
        lines = [
            f"ESC Meeting: {self.meeting_date.isoformat()}",
            f"Attendees: {', '.join(self.attendees)}",
            "",
            f"Decisions taken ({len(self.decisions)}):",
        ]
        for d in self.decisions:
            lines.append(f"  ✓ {d}")
        lines.append("")
        lines.append(f"Action items ({len(self.action_items)}):")
        for a in self.action_items:
            lines.append(f"  [{a.status.value:10}] {a.description[:50]:50} → {a.owner} by {a.due_date}")
        return "\n".join(lines)

# Example ESC meeting record
q2_meeting = ESCMeeting(
    meeting_date=date(2025, 6, 15),
    attendees=["CTO (Chair)", "CFO", "CISO", "CLO", "BU Finance GM", "BU Legal GM", "CoE Lead (observer)"],
    decisions=[
        "Phase 2 gate: ADVANCE — all 5 gate criteria met; Phase 3 budget of $2.1M approved",
        "UC-003 (Credit Decisioning AI): APPROVED — Tier 3 with mandatory human-in-loop checkpoint and monthly risk review",
        "UC-005 (Automated Audit): REJECTED — output constitutes regulated opinion; resubmit with human-review checkpoint",
    ],
    action_items=[
        ActionItem("A-001", "Publish Phase 3 intake process to BU leads", "CoE Lead", date(2025, 6, 30)),
        ActionItem("A-002", "Draft UC-003 monitoring runbook for CISO review", "CoE Platform Lead", date(2025, 7, 15)),
        ActionItem("A-003", "CFO to release Phase 3 budget tranche 1 to CoE cost centre", "CFO", date(2025, 6, 20)),
        ActionItem("A-004", "CLO to engage external counsel on EU AI Act classification for UC-003", "CLO", date(2025, 7, 31)),
    ],
)

print(q2_meeting.meeting_pack_summary())

# Check overdue at a later date
review_date = date(2025, 7, 5)
overdue = q2_meeting.overdue_actions(review_date)
print(f"\nOverdue at {review_date}: {len(overdue)} item(s)")
for a in overdue:
    print(f"  OVERDUE: {a.description} (due {a.due_date}, owner: {a.owner})")
The ESC receives a standardised reporting package 5 business days before each quarterly meeting. The package has four components:
One-page summary: top 3 wins, top 3 risks, key decisions needed, programme RAG status (Red/Amber/Green)
Active use cases, measured impact YTD vs plan, platform SLA, cost vs budget, intake pipeline
Open risk items by tier; incidents in the quarter; regulatory developments
One paper per decision on the agenda; includes recommendation, rationale, and options considered
The programme RAG (Red/Amber/Green) status reported on the executive summary is determined by the most severe status across four dimensions:
1–2 milestones > 2 weeks behind; recovery plan in place
> 2 milestones behind OR critical path milestone at risk
Spend > 20% off plan OR forecast overrun without approved remedy
Measured impact 50–80% of plan; remediation plan active
Measured impact < 50% of plan OR flagship use case in Under Review state
No open P1 incidents; no new regulatory exposures
Open P2 incidents; regulatory enquiry in progress but managed
Open P1 incident; active regulatory enforcement; CISO suspension active

# Programme RAG status calculator
from dataclasses import dataclass
from enum import Enum

class RAGStatus(Enum):
    GREEN = "Green"
    AMBER = "Amber"
    RED   = "Red"

@dataclass
class ProgrammeRAG:
    # Schedule
    h1_milestones_behind: int
    critical_path_at_risk: bool
    # Budget
    spend_variance_pct: float     # actual/plan - 1 (positive = over budget)
    forecast_overrun_unremedied: bool
    # Impact
    impact_vs_plan_pct: float     # measured / planned (0.0–1.0+)
    flagship_use_case_under_review: bool
    # Risk
    open_p1_incidents: int
    active_regulatory_enforcement: bool
    ciso_suspension_active: bool

    def schedule_rag(self) -> RAGStatus:
        if self.critical_path_at_risk or self.h1_milestones_behind > 2:
            return RAGStatus.RED
        if self.h1_milestones_behind > 0:
            return RAGStatus.AMBER
        return RAGStatus.GREEN

    def budget_rag(self) -> RAGStatus:
        abs_var = abs(self.spend_variance_pct)
        if abs_var > 0.20 or self.forecast_overrun_unremedied:
            return RAGStatus.RED
        if abs_var > 0.10:
            return RAGStatus.AMBER
        return RAGStatus.GREEN

    def impact_rag(self) -> RAGStatus:
        if self.impact_vs_plan_pct < 0.50 or self.flagship_use_case_under_review:
            return RAGStatus.RED
        if self.impact_vs_plan_pct < 0.80:
            return RAGStatus.AMBER
        return RAGStatus.GREEN

    def risk_rag(self) -> RAGStatus:
        if self.open_p1_incidents > 0 or self.active_regulatory_enforcement or self.ciso_suspension_active:
            return RAGStatus.RED
        return RAGStatus.GREEN

    def overall(self) -> RAGStatus:
        statuses = [self.schedule_rag(), self.budget_rag(), self.impact_rag(), self.risk_rag()]
        if RAGStatus.RED in statuses:
            return RAGStatus.RED
        if RAGStatus.AMBER in statuses:
            return RAGStatus.AMBER
        return RAGStatus.GREEN

    def summary(self) -> str:
        return (
            f"Schedule: {self.schedule_rag().value} | "
            f"Budget: {self.budget_rag().value} | "
            f"Impact: {self.impact_rag().value} | "
            f"Risk: {self.risk_rag().value} | "
            f"Overall: {self.overall().value}"
        )

rag = ProgrammeRAG(
    h1_milestones_behind=1,
    critical_path_at_risk=False,
    spend_variance_pct=0.08,
    forecast_overrun_unremedied=False,
    impact_vs_plan_pct=0.72,
    flagship_use_case_under_review=False,
    open_p1_incidents=0,
    active_regulatory_enforcement=False,
    ciso_suspension_active=False,
)
print(rag.summary())
# Schedule: Amber | Budget: Green | Impact: Amber | Risk: Green | Overall: Amber
Decisions get overruled by other C-suite; BU GMs do not feel accountable to committee
Chair must be CTO, CDO, or CEO; CEO sponsorship is optimal even if CTO chairs operationally
Meetings produce updates and discussion but no binding outcomes; decisions drift back to CoE Lead or informal channels
Every agenda item is either a decision (with a vote) or information (explicitly labelled as such); prohibit hybrid 'discussion for awareness' items
BU interests not represented; BU disputes escalate outside the committee
Attendance is a programme commitment for BU GMs, not optional; send pre-read materials 5 business days early to reduce preparation burden
Tier 3 risk approvals are made without CISO or Legal input; regulatory exposures not surfaced until after launch
CISO and CLO are standing members, not invitees; their attendance is non-negotiable for Tier 3 agenda items
Committee spends time on pilot status updates instead of programme strategy; members disengage
Report operational detail in the dashboard; ESC agenda covers only decisions, risks, and strategy. CoE Lead filters operational items.

The ESC should be constituted by a formal charter document — not an email or a verbal agreement. The charter establishes the committee's authority, membership, quorum rules, and decision-making process. Without a charter, the committee's authority is informal and can be overridden when convenient.
Committee name and reporting relationship (e.g. reports to CEO; provides quarterly briefing to Board audit committee)
Membership: named roles, not named individuals (roles change; the committee continues)
Chair: named role; deputy chair (covers when chair is unavailable)
Quorum: minimum members required for a decision to be binding (typically Chair + 2 voting members for Tier 1-2 decisions; Chair + CISO + CLO for Tier 3)
Decision process: how votes are taken; how ties are broken; what constitutes a passed motion
Cadence: quarterly meetings; extraordinary session trigger criteria and convening authority
Term: the charter should be reviewed annually and updated if programme phase changes
Amendment: how the charter itself can be changed (requires ESC vote)

A steering committee charter that has been reviewed by Legal and signed by the Chair is 10× more durable than one that exists as a shared slide deck. The signing is not ceremony — it is the moment the committee's members accept the governance obligations in writing.
The executive steering committee converts ad hoc politics into a defined governance mechanism — without it, contested decisions stall or resolve inconsistently
Optimal membership: 5–8 voting members including CTO/CDO (Chair), CFO, CISO, CLO, and rotating BU GMs; CoE Lead attends as a non-voting observer
Documented decision rights matrix is mandatory: every contested decision category must have a named decision maker and named advisory inputs
Quarterly cadence for strategy and phase gates; extraordinary sessions for incidents, enforcement, and major programme crises
Reporting package arrives 5 business days before each meeting: executive summary, performance dashboard, risk register, and decision papers (one per agenda decision)
RAG status covers four dimensions: schedule, budget, business impact, and risk; any Red dimension makes the programme overall Red
Common failure modes: junior chair, no formal voting, absent risk function, committee reviewing operational details — all preventable with a properly constituted charter
